title: Gilia mitracarpa
taxonomy: Gilia mitracarpa is classified as a member of the plant family Polemoniaceae. This family is commonly known as the Phlox or Jacob's-ladder family. Gilia mitracarpa is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the genus Gilia, which is a group of annual or biennial plants native to North America. The genus Gilia is further classified within the order Ericales, which includes a diverse range of flowering plant families. The classification of Gilia mitracarpa is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Asterids.
uses: Gilia mitracarpa, also known as the shrubby gilia, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its leaves and flowers are used to make a tea that is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. This tea is used to treat conditions such as sore throats, coughs, and digestive issues. Additionally, the plant's roots and stems are used to make a poultice that is applied topically to treat skin irritations and wounds. More research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Gilia mitracarpa for medicinal purposes.

intro: Gilia mitracarpa, also known as Mountain Balm, is a perennial herb belonging to the Polemoniaceae family, native to the mountainous regions of North America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and expectorant effects, which may help alleviate respiratory issues and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Gilia mitracarpa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and salves, which are used to treat respiratory issues, skin conditions, and wounds.
